serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of pig intestine used in cooking is approximately 100g, equivalent to a portion of cleaned intestine before braising, stir-frying, or boiling.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (24mg/100g) in raw state; however potassium is also very low (18mg/100g) providing negligible vasodilatory offset
hypertension.guidance: Pig intestine is low in sodium in its raw state and is acceptable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added sauces; however, standard braising preparations using soy sauce, oyster sauce, or fermented bean paste can raise sodium to 400–700mg/100g, making sauce-free or minimal-seasoning preparation essential for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate supports glycaemic stability, but high total fat (16.6g/100g) and high cholesterol (154mg/100g) elevate cardiovascular risk in T2D patients
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pig intestine contains no carbohydrate and will not raise blood glucose directly; however, its high fat content (16.6g/100g) may slow gastric emptying and its high cholesterol (154mg/100g) warrants monitoring of lipid profiles in T2D patients who already carry elevated cardiovascular risk — consume in moderation and avoid sugar-based braising sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol (154mg/100g), high total fat (16.6g/100g), saturated fat (~6.0g/100g), and trace trans fat (0.1g/100g) are all atherogenic risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pig intestine is high in dietary cholesterol (154mg/100g), saturated fat (~6.0g/100g), and contains trace trans fat (0.1g/100g), all of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and avoid high-sodium preparation methods that compound vascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~6.0g/100g) exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold; trace trans fat (0.1g/100g) is pro-inflammatory; pork-derived arachidonic acid contributes to joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pig intestine is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat content exceeding the RA threshold, the presence of trace trans fat which is pro-inflammatory at any level, and its pork-derived arachidonic acid content which promotes prostaglandin-mediated joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pig intestine, raw (also known as pork intestine or pig gut) is the uncooked large intestine of the domestic pig, presenting as a pale, tubular organ with a characteristic fatty lining. In its raw state it has a mild, slightly gamey odour and a soft, pliable texture that firms and becomes chewy upon cooking. It is a widely consumed offal ingredient across Chinese, Southeast Asian, and Singaporean cuisines, where it is most commonly prepared by thorough cleaning, blanching, and then braising in soy sauce, five-spice, and aromatics to produce dishes such as braised pig intestine (卤猪大肠) or stir-fried intestine with ginger and spring onion. Per 100g in the raw state, pig intestine provides 182 kcal, 7.64g protein, 16.61g fat (including approximately 6.0g saturated fat and trace trans fat at 0.1g), and 0g carbohydrate, making it a high-fat, zero-carbohydrate protein source. Cholesterol is notably high at 154mg/100g. Sodium is very low at 24mg/100g in the raw state, though preparation methods typically increase sodium substantially. Clinically, pig intestine is not suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol content. It may be consumed in moderation by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension provided preparation avoids high-sodium sauces and sugar-based marinades. It is not halal and not kosher.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06090015) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is a primary approved regional database for Singapore food composition. The match is exact — the HPB entry is specifically for pig intestine, raw.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — pig intestine in its raw state contains no carbohydrate by composition; this is naturally zero for animal intestinal tissue. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per the same source; naturally zero for this animal-tissue food product. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as intestinal tissue contains no plant-derived fibre; naturally zero. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference data for pork intestine (item 10193) used to supplement the HPB record which does not separately report trans fat; this value is documented as an adapted estimate. Saturated_fat_g of approximately 6.0g/100g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork intestine data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for raw pig intestine is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this item and are not available from a directly matching approved source entry as of 2026. Glycemic index is not applicable as pig intestine contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g; glycemic_index is set to the numeric sentinel value 0 to satisfy schema numeric requirements, gi_category is set to not_applicable, and gi_source documents the rationale. Pig intestine is not halal (pork product) and not kosher. The food is consumed in Singapore primarily in Chinese culinary contexts (kway chap, braised offal dishes). Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi in accordance with Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirements.
